description: Biology grounding for the biology/health lens — molecular, cellular, evolutionary, and systems principles.

entries.title: The central dogma: DNA → RNA → protein
entries.summary: Genetic information flows DNA → (transcription) → mRNA → (translation) → protein, with the genetic code mapping nucleotide triplets (codons) to amino acids. DNA is the stable archive; RNA the working copy; protein the functional machine. Reverse transcription (RNA→DNA, retroviruses) and regulation at every step (epigenetics, splicing) refine the dogma without overturning it.

entries.title: Evolution by natural selection
entries.summary: Heritable variation + differential reproductive success = adaptation over generations. Traits that raise fitness in an environment become more common; there is no foresight, only selection on existing variation (plus drift, mutation, gene flow). It explains the unity and diversity of life and underlies antibiotic resistance, crop breeding, and evolutionary algorithms in ML.

entries.title: Cell theory & membranes
entries.summary: All life is made of cells, the smallest unit of life, arising only from existing cells. The phospholipid bilayer membrane (hydrophilic heads out, hydrophobic tails in) creates the inside/outside boundary and controls transport. Eukaryotic cells compartmentalize functions in organelles (nucleus, mitochondria, ER); prokaryotes don't — the deepest division in the tree of life.

entries.title: Cellular respiration & ATP
entries.summary: Cells extract energy from glucose through glycolysis, the citric-acid cycle, and oxidative phosphorylation, storing it in ATP — the universal energy currency. The electron transport chain pumps protons to drive ATP synthase (chemiosmosis). Photosynthesis runs the reverse logic, capturing light to build glucose; together they power nearly the entire biosphere.

entries.title: Enzymes & catalysis
entries.summary: Enzymes are protein catalysts that lower a reaction's activation energy, speeding it enormously without being consumed, with exquisite substrate specificity (lock-and-key / induced fit). Michaelis–Menten kinetics describe the rate vs substrate concentration (Vmax, Km); inhibitors (competitive/non-competitive) modulate them — the basis of much pharmacology.

entries.title: Homeostasis & feedback
entries.summary: Organisms hold internal variables (temperature, pH, glucose, osmolarity) within narrow ranges via negative feedback loops — a sensor, a set point, and effectors that oppose deviation (e.g. insulin/glucagon for blood sugar). Positive feedback is rarer and used for committed events (childbirth, action potentials). This is biology's version of the control-theory feedback in the robotics lens.

entries.title: The immune system: innate & adaptive
entries.summary: Innate immunity is fast, general first defense (barriers, phagocytes, inflammation); adaptive immunity is slower but specific and remembers — B cells make antibodies, T cells kill infected cells and coordinate. Immunological memory is why we recover faster on re-exposure and why vaccines work: they train adaptive immunity without the disease.

entries.title: Neurons & action potentials
entries.summary: Neurons signal with action potentials — all-or-nothing voltage spikes from ion (Na⁺/K⁺) flux across the membrane — that propagate down the axon and release neurotransmitters at synapses to excite or inhibit the next cell. Networks of these binary-ish spikes compute; the artificial neuron of deep learning is a (very loose) abstraction of this integrate-and-fire logic.

entries.title: Ecosystems, niches & carrying capacity
entries.summary: Energy flows one way through trophic levels (producers → consumers → decomposers), losing ~90% at each step, while matter cycles. A population grows logistically toward its environment's carrying capacity K; predator–prey dynamics oscillate (Lotka–Volterra). A species' niche is its role + requirements; competitive exclusion forbids two species occupying the identical niche indefinitely.

entries.title: Mendelian & population genetics
entries.summary: Genes come in alleles; offspring inherit one from each parent, with dominant alleles masking recessive ones (Mendel's segregation and independent assortment). At the population scale, Hardy–Weinberg equilibrium predicts allele frequencies absent selection/drift/migration/mutation — the null model whose violation signals evolution in action.
